title: Design Patterns (Observer, Factory, Singleton)
summary: Design patterns are general, reusable solutions to common coding problems.
They come from all of programming, not just frontend.
Examples include Observer, Factory, and Singleton.
Knowing them helps you recognize and discuss solutions.

sections.text: Beyond components, programming as a whole has a famous set of reusable solutions called design patterns. They are language independent ideas for structuring code, and a few names come up so often that they are worth knowing.

sections.title: What Design Patterns Are
sections.text: A design pattern is a general, proven solution to a problem that shows up across all kinds of software. It is not code you copy, but a shape you apply. They were collected and named decades ago, and the names became a shared language developers use to describe how code is organized.

sections.title: Three You Will Hear
sections.text: A few names recur. The Observer pattern lets many parts listen and react when something changes, much like the event listeners you already used. The Factory pattern is a tidy way to create objects. The Singleton ensures there is only ever one of something. You do not need to master all patterns, but recognizing these helps.

media.alt: Observer, Factory, and Singleton shown as three named code shapes

sections.title: Recipes in a Cookbook
sections.text: Design patterns are like recipes in a shared cookbook. Each one is a proven approach to a common dish, with a known name. You adapt it to your ingredients rather than copying exactly. And when a cook mentions a recipe by name, everyone in the kitchen knows roughly what they mean.

media.alt: A shared cookbook of named recipes adapted to different ingredients

sections.title: Working with Design Patterns
items: Recognize a problem common across software.
Recall if a named pattern fits it.
Apply the pattern's shape to your own code.
Use its name to communicate with other developers.
Learn the common ones first, not all at once.

sections.text: The Observer pattern is exactly what is happening every time you add an event listener. Many patterns describe things you already do, just giving them a formal name.

terms.definition: A general, reusable solution to a coding problem.
A pattern where parts react to changes.
A tidy pattern for creating objects.
A pattern ensuring only one of something.
Pattern names developers use to communicate.

options: General, reusable solutions to common coding problems
